text: I am a trainman for the railroad who has been on the ground as well as in the cab for 5 years now and I have been observed working and tested many times by upper management without a single blemish on my record. The first time I was drone tested I was caught off guard and lost focus for a moment of the task at hand and in this line of work that is extremely dangerous.  Drones are not something you can get use to either when being tested, they will always grab your attention at some point versus someone standing afar watching you work or from a vehicle, they also pose other risks as well. Drones pose severe safety risks when flown over active railroad yards due to the high potential for catastrophic operational interference. A drone crash on or near tracks can damage sensitive track switches, signal systems, or overhead electrical catenary wires, leading to train derailments or widespread power failures. The presence of an unauthorized drone distracts locomotive engineers and yard workers who are operating heavy tonnage equipment, creating fatal blind spots in an environment requiring absolute situational awareness. Furthermore, drone radio frequencies can interfere with the wireless remote-control systems used to shunt railcars, potentially causing unguided train movements. Finally, many rail yards transport hazardous materials, meaning a drone impact or a dropped battery sparks a direct risk of explosions or toxic chemical spills. Thanks for reading.

What I am trying to understand is, what are these drones for??? Are the drones being used to "stop the line". Is this a way to help keep railroad employees safe??? Is the purpose to use it to test employees in critical situations. Should this not be a teachable moment. Are these drones distractions in an already fragile environment!!! You are creating an unsafe situation.

I am strongly opposed to this, I have been a railroad photographer using drones for years. Legally and safely, this would kill unique aspects of photographer that capture the diverse landscapes and scenes of America and beyond that aren’t reachable on the ground. Railroads utilize drones as well for similar purposes of surveillance and photographer, used in many of their promotional and media. This would lead to fragmented airspace that would be incredibly difficult to understand and navigate for hobbyists and small businesses. It is already difficult enough with many local agencies having misinformation about regulations.

Critical infrastructure sites such as hospitals, trauma centers and clinics operate in a highly regulated field that bears heavy burden of accreditation loss for irresponsible handling of confidential patient information and appropriate privacy practices. As drone technology becomes more widely available and more efficient; these institutions face a much higher risk factor of exploitation with potentially huge ramifications that could impact population's ability to seek care. This is even more so in rural areas that have significantly less options for local care.

Another major risk factor in the rise of commercial and private drone usage is operation of designated landing zones. Healthcare systems typically see substantial helicopter traffic for higher care needs. With unregulated drones occupying airspace and no ability to regulate or enforce- healthcare facilities run a risk of accidents that effect critical patient transportation and individuals on site.

Healthcare institutions need the ability to restrict air space to safely coordinate safe landing spaces and provide a reasonable expectation of privacy to patients they serve. Air space should be restricted to providers of medical transportation and require any other entitles to acquire written permission with specific times and locations of drone operation to operate safely and appropriately. Institutions should also have the ability to enforce this regulation immediately to ensure preservation of life in critical care cases that cannot wait. As drones grow in commonality, technology also has entered with capabilities to keep accountability and safety now and into the evolving future.

Restricting drones in a rail yard needs to be enforced for the safety of their labor workers. Plenty of accidents happen in the yard due to distractions the last thing we need is another distraction. Yard masters already have cameras every where, the point of having drones are so Managers can play with toys like little kids instead of focusing on true safety and production.

Hello. I’ve worked at Union Pacific Railroad for 27yrs as as a conductor switchmen. Flying drones in rail yards is dangerous and irresponsible. Rail yards are filled with heavy equipment, moving trains, and hazardous materials that create serious safety risks. Drones can distract workers and interfere with operations. Using a drone to watch someone without their permission is also an invasion of privacy. Respect safety rules, workers, and other people’s privacy. Thanks.

commenter: Comment from Milwaukee Metropolitan Sewerage District
text:
May 14, 2026

Docket Operations; U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT)
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Room W58-213, West Building 5th Floor
Washington, DC 20590-0001

Re: Docket number FAA-2026-4558

To whom it may concern:

I am writing on behalf of the Milwaukee Metropolitan Sewerage District to voice the District's support for the proposed rule: Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility, Published Document 2026-08943 (91 FR 24650). The proposed action would, inter alia, implement section 2209, of the FAA Extension, Safety and Security Act of 2016, by establishing a process for operators and proprietors of certain fixed site facilities to request and maintain an unmanned aircraft flight restriction.

The District is a state-chartered government entity with a mission to protect public health, property, and the environment by providing wastewater conveyance, treatment, and flood management services within a certain territory. This territory includes virtually all the cities and villages in Milwaukee County, and all or part of nine municipalities in Ozaukee, Racine, Washington, and Waukesha counties. MMSD currently serves 29 municipalities, about 1.1 million people, and countless businesses.

The District has two wastewater treatment facilities, the Jones Island Water Reclamation Facility in the City of Milwaukee and the South Shore Water Reclamation Facility located in Oak Creek, Wisconsin. With a rise in security concerns emanating from home and abroad, the District takes all available measures to ensure the security of its treatment plants and headquarters facility. Moreover, the District has taken a keen interest in the threats posed by unmanned aircraft (drones). The District was at the forefront of recent legislative changes in Wisconsin (2025 Wis Act 194) to regulate the operation of drones over critical District facilities, as well as those critical assets of other utility providers (electric, water, gas, telecom, etc.).

The threat is not merely hypothetical. The District recently suffered a security breach when a drone of unknown origin crashed onto the Jones Island Water Reclamation Facility, which triggered a security response and ultimately led to Homeland Security taking possession of the crashed drone for forensic analysis. Incidents like this are at the forefront of the minds of District staff responsible for ensuring safe operation of our facilities and the safety of our personnel.

Because of the challenges posed by federal preemption of drone operation over US airspace, it can be confusing as to who is ultimately responsible for ensuring the safety of our staff and the more than one million residents who depend upon us to deliver service 24 hours a day, 365 days a year, from the grave risk posed by a malevolent drone operator. The District strongly supports the proposed rule as a key part of a comprehensive effort to ensure critical public utilities may take action to regulate drone use around those critical assets, and that those efforts will be supported both at the state and federal levels.

The technology is evolving faster than the law, and this proposal demonstrates a concerted effort to ensure critical utility providers can take all available measures to protect their assets from the risk of harm posed by unmanned aircraft.

Sincerely,

Joseph T. Ganzer
Senior Staff Attorney
Milwaukee Metropolitan Sewerage District

Drones in rail yards or anywhere on the railroad is a bad idea unless its for accident investigations after a derail they should not be allowed to use drones in observation of crews at any time it causes distractions and serious safety concerns

commenter: Comment from Corona Norco Unified School District
text: As a Director of Safety & Security in a large urban school district in Southern California, we often receive calls about drones flying over schools, students, and staff. We've also experienced nefarious drones dropping items onto the campuses of schools.  We would like the ability to limit drone/UAS flights over schools to approved activities.

Conductor/Engineer

Local Safety and Legislative Representative

I have eight years of service.

The flying of drones by management to observe and test crews has had a very negative impact on the safety and moral of crews while trying to perform their task. I ask that the FFA grounds these types of aggressive testing practices and let our crews get our critical work done with minimal distractions.

I am a freelance photographer who photographs the railroad both commercially and as a hobby. I am in the process of acquiring my Part 107 certification to allow myself to add drone photography to my commercial offerings. I am writing to give my opposition to the Designation—Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility in its current form.

Currently, as the rule is written, the railroad would be able to blanketly ban operations of small, personal drones in airspace over any of their property for hobby use. The drone operations of myself and my fellow hobbyists is not meant to interfere with railroad operations or trespass on railroad property and all FAA regulations are followed during flight operations. Myself and my fellow photographers pride ourselves on safe, respectful operations that aim to document the history of rail travel in this country while being respectful to the crews and railroad property.

Under the current proposal, railroads can request flight restrictions for unmanned aircraft over "fixed-sight facilities", and the FAA asks whether or not the restrictions should extend beyond the property lines. While that could make sense in other scenarios, the concern with implementing those restrictions over railroad property is the sheer length and direction that the tracks follow, often paralleling public roadways and areas where it is legal to watch the trains go by.

While the proposed rule includes provisions to allow operations, they focus mostly on commercial and government use and do not include provisions to protect the operations of hobbyists operating with small unmanned aircraft. Because hobbyists are not protected, the railroads would be given the ability to prevent drone operation not just in areas of security risk but as a blanket ban of aerial photography of railroad property and operations. If that were to happen, a hobby for many railfans across the country would be outlawed for seemingly no legitimate reason.

I urge the FAA to reject the rule as it is currently proposed and to restructure the rule to allow protection for those using small unmanned drones for railroad photography in areas that are not deemed as security threats and allow for the continued hobby for many across the country to continue in a safe, respectful and lawful manner. Thank you.

Hello, I am a locomotive engineer with 21 years of service.  Before I was an engineer, I worked in Tilford yard (Atlanta) as both an RCO operator and yard foreman.  I can assure anyone that has never been in a switching environment, be it flat or hump, or at a customer location, that the life and limb threatening operations that occur moving 80k plus pound cars around require focus, and all distractions, be it a manager in person or flying a drone are dangerous and unwelcome.  Combine this with the fact that all class 1 railroads have a huge influx of workers with less than 5 years experience, and drones can only lead to negative outcomes.  As a professional railroader, we are the experts on safety in our working environment.  Not someone watching from 100 feet above.

Train Engineer and conductor.

Rail yards are already dangerous enough without drones creating distractions overhead. Railroad workers are the true experts on rail safety.

Drones used for surveillance and operational testing create unnecessary hazards and security concerns.

Railroad workers deserve to be consulted before these practices are normalized in critical infrastructure environments.

Normalizing drones flying over the thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals can’t be allowed to happen, because we won’t know when we are seeing an act of terrorism in progress vs. an MTO trying to conduct O-Tests.

From:
Conductor for CSX FOR 15 years
Home Terminal: Cincinnati, OH
Railyards are inherently dangerous 24/7/52. Every minute of every day there is potential for, at a minimum, great bodily harm, if not death from 150 Ton moving railcars, and/or 250 Ton moving locomotives. Railroad management, however, sees it as necessary to fly a drone to enforce safety rules and policy from a desk or a truck. Most managers have never worked in the field. They do not realize the grave situation that is caused by raliroaders trying to navigate the perils of the job while looking up at the sky for a drone! Twisted ankles, torn knees, and falls are just a few dangers. Railroaders are constantly being rushed and questioned and watched, etc etc, and that is without drones. Now we have an additional source of stress with drones, and what they bring. Terrorist surveillance? Who knows, there is no way to know. We cannot continue to be placed in danger because a manager is lazy and has no people skills to come out and observe in the light. It is ridiculous!

Conductor -20 years of service
Drones are and have been a complete and utter distraction while trying to perform our job ,our job is dangerous enough and to have flying objects over our heads creates an even more dangerous environment.

I have 38 years in the railroad industry as a Conductor. Active rail yards are dangerous to begin with and having drones flying around causes a unsafe condition. Front line railroad workers are true experts on rail safety. Drones used for surveillance and operational testing create unnecessary hazards and safety concerns. Railroad workers should be consulted before these practices are normalized in critical infrastructure environments. Normalizing drones flying over thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals can't be allowed to happen, because we don't know if we are seeing an act of terrorism or an MTO trying to conduct operational testing.

As a switchperson of 13 years at Alfalfa Yard in El Paso, TX for Union Pacific Railroad, to me active rail yards are already dangerous enough without drones creating distractions overhead.
Don't be deceive; drones are not used for safety but rather used for surveillance and operational testing creating an unnecessary hazard and security concern for us on the ground.
Drone use should not normalized in critical infrastructure environments.
Normalizing drones flying over in train yards, disctracting crews working handling the thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals can’t be allowed to happen. When we see a drone, we won’t know if it is an influcuencer recording the yard, an MYO trying to test crews or God forbid an act of terrorism in progress.

From my experience at UPRR as a conductor since 2004, the company has hired a lot of people then think they over hired and then used tools like droans to fire people. With that being said I’m sure most managers flying them are breaking an FAA rule or tow every time they fly them, but those rule don’t apply to them. Double standard for sure. They fly them out of line of sight all the time and when you do see them you have no idea if it’s management or someone else not associated with our operations.

I work for a short-line railroad in the Port of Stockton and I have 4 years on the job. Like other ports in the nation, we are a MARSEC level 1 secure facility and not open to the public. There are specific rules regulating drone usage at the port. Specifically, the drone operator must be licensed and give 36 hour notice to the port police before flying. Today, as I am writing this, was the first day we saw a drone at the port. We knew who it was and we knew why they were flying the drone: it was our new superintendent watching us work. But nobody told us anyone had permission to fly today and our instinct was to notify our supervisors and the port police. When we notified our supervisors, they too were unaware of anyone using a drone, sanctioned or otherwise. We’re used to this sort of behavior from our superiors: keep us in the dark, obfuscate the truth and their intentions, and keep us guessing. We are professionals and we know how to deal with these circumstances in a productive and safe manner.

There are others at the port who are not as trained as we are. They aren’t railroaders. They are the other men and women who make the port work. They aren’t used to being surveilled and they take the security of the port very seriously.

While switching railcars for a specific customer today, we were working under the direction of an employee of that customer. I will call him Mike. Mike informed me that the drone “buzzed” his head and he was very concerned for not only his safety, but the operational security of the port as well. He found me and pointed to the drone. “Is that yours?” he asked and I replied in the affirmative. It was, again, the first time either one of us had seen the drone. When Mike told me about how close it got to him and expressed his concerns for safety, I implored him to notify his management and spare no detail.

After our safety briefing was done, Mike and I continued with our work and I watched the drone. I watched it go down to the ground level, stopping in front of personal vehicles and commercial vehicles, back up again, and down again. This not only distracted us, but distracted Mike and the other carriers trying to do their work as well (offloading material from railcars into tractor trailers), and in this environment, where we all look out for each other; where we collaborate safely to deliver product to our customers and keep these truck drivers (carriers) moving without delay, we all stopped—and looked at this drone bob up and down. It appeared to be piloted by an amateur.

How embarrassing this was for me and my team. Our management’s cavalier attitude towards the safety of working men and women in the port who aren’t railroaders is unacceptable. All our management team cares about is watching us. They have no regard for anyone else, like Mike, when they buzz right over the top of our heads and fly haphazardly all in some search for a rules violation.

I hereby swear the the above testimony is true to my knowledge and if you don’t believe me, I invite you to subpoena the drone footage from the Port of Stockton, filmed on today’s date, May 13, 2026 at around 11:30 am. Thank you.

I believe there should be restrictions in flying over jails, prisons, court houses, police stations, fire stations, and other critical infrastructure type facilities. However I believe the language in the law should allow the administrators of such buildings to be able to give permission to persons to fly and I believe language should include that well established news media entities should be able to fly but must give some type of notice when doing so. The language for well established media should be able to describe an actual mainstream media organization with legitimate journalists and not these whacky first amendment auditors that call their selves journalists.

I believe such restrictions should also include public schools.

I also believe the restrictions on shooting drones should be loosened. Firing on a drone is not at all the same as firing on an aircraft with souls. These places of critical infrastructure should be able to shoot drones down without too much federal red tape. If they do it legally per state law and don't endanger others then they should be able to shoot a drone down. I also believe that private property owners should be able to shoot drones down if a drone is hovering over their home and being an obvious harassment and nuisance. There is no legitimate reason for anyone to fly a drone into someone's back yard and let it hover there for several minutes. A person should be able to take action on the trespassing drone so long as they are being safe about it and not endangering others.

Going on 12 years in the indutry in some capacity and now a Locomotive Engineer I have unforuntatly seen my fair share of distractions and accidents due to outside distractions. Currently my local Safety Representative I come to you with this thought.  The use of Drones in Railroad Yards that are used for testing and monitoring purposes are an extreme distraction to the workers on the ground. They need to be restricted for inspection, overseeing accident scenes, etc. But not for use in the yards across the United States. Most accidents in our industry occur during switching operations and the last thing we need is more distraction from around or above. If this government truly believes in safety and that stands on the side of the labor force moving America's goods we need to be sure and delegate and restrict the use of drones in and around Railraod Yards.

As a railroad conductor with approximately 2 1/2 years of experience working in active rail yards, I strongly believe drones have no place operating over switching operations, train movements, and hazardous work environments without serious oversight and input from frontline railroad employees.

Rail yards are already high-risk environments that require constant communication, situational awareness, and concentration between crews on the ground, locomotive engineers, utility employees, and dispatching personnel. Adding drones overhead creates another layer of distraction and introduces unnecessary safety concerns during critical operations.

As a newer railroader, I believe this issue is especially important. Many new employees are already working through the pressures and anxieties that come with learning the craft in an environment where one mistake can have serious consequences. Due to the lack of proper training and the steep learning curve many new railroaders face, our focus needs to remain completely locked in on our surroundings, radio communication, train movements, and instructions from our crews. Drones operating overhead only add another distraction in an environment where our attention already has to be spot on at all times.

I have personally witnessed trainmasters and management personnel operating drones near active crews and switching operations. In many cases, these drones are used for surveillance, efficiency monitoring, or operational testing rather than true safety purposes. This creates tension, distraction, and uncertainty for crews already performing dangerous work around moving equipment, heavy machinery, and hazardous materials.

Railroad workers are the true rail safety experts because we are the individuals physically performing the work every day. Decisions involving drone operations in rail yards should not be normalized without direct consultation from the workers exposed to these risks.

There are also serious security concerns regarding drones flying over critical infrastructure and trains carrying hazardous chemicals. Workers on the ground should never have to question whether a drone overhead is being used for operational testing or represents a legitimate security threat. In today’s environment, normalizing unidentified drone activity around hazardous materials and transportation infrastructure is dangerous and irresponsible.

The FAA should carefully consider the concerns of railroad employees before allowing widespread drone use in active rail operations.

In solidarity,
Monica Jolene Thompson

As a railroad employee, the idea of more surveillance is wild.   We currently are under 24hr surveillance in the yards.    The idea of drones flying around causing a physical distraction is wild.   The mental aspect of constant supervision is hard enough, let alone worry about a drone flying around

I've been in the rail industry for 27 years. I think the drones will create a massive distraction for employees in the field. We already have enough to keep us distracted and this will only complicate things further. A conductor or Engineer has enough to worry about. This is not safe by any means.
Drones are incredibly distracting while performing work in a rail yard. Please ban their use in rail yards.

I have been an active conductor for 22 years with Union Pacific Railroad and understand the dangers we face every day in the railroading industry. Having another distraction like drone activity would add another layer of potential danger to our jobs. Every day we handle highly hazardous materials that pose a serious risk to ourselves, our fellow employees and the communities around us. The last thing we need is a drone that could possibly take our attention away from our tasks safely moving the nations freight. Dealing with thousands of tons of equipment requires our utmost attention.

Been working for the railroad for almost 5 years and the dangers of drones flying over head is distracting to conductors riding/watching shoving moves being a railroader is stressful enough with out having flying cameras to dictate and watch your every move having a stress free work environment is safer for everyone

Railroad employees are the first line of defense for detecting trespassers and bad actors. Normalizing drones in areas that drones should not be allowed in, makes reporting confusing or seem even unnecessary.
The drones are a dangerous distraction while shoving

Re: 14 CFR Parts 1, 74, 91, and 107
[Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03]
RIN 2120-AL33

To Whom It May Concern:

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the authorization of drone activities within the physical confines of the United States transportation infrastructure. As a professional in the industry with 29 years of service, I urge regulatory bodies to evaluate this issue through the lens of national security rather than corporate convenience or cost reduction. The risks of drone deployment in transport environments outweigh the perceived operational benefits for three critical reasons:

1. Vulnerability to Bad Actors: Global conflicts demonstrate that drones are easily weaponized by nefarious groups. Concurrently, our physical and digital transportation infrastructure faces ongoing threats from sophisticated cyber actors. Introducing routine drone usage creates an optimal environment for adversaries to exploit.

2. Employee Confusion and Security Breaches: Workforce personnel cannot reliably distinguish between authorized commercial drones and unauthorized surveillance or weaponized devices. Even if employees are notified of scheduled drone operations, they may misidentify a hostile drone as an official asset. This creates a dangerous security blind spot.

3. Compromising Supply-Line Integrity: Protecting critical supply lines requires physical oversight. Replacing human vigilance with automated aerial systems undercuts our defense posture. Supply-line disruption is the primary objective of hostile forces, and we must not facilitate this risk in the name of labor reduction.

Regulatory oversight must look beyond immediate corporate cost savings. This is a matter of national security and critical infrastructure protection. I wholly urge the government to maintain strict prohibitions on drone operations across all transportation routes, facilities, and yards.

Sincerely,

David Liddle
Regional Insurance Manager
SMART-TD / AJFL

Drones should be banned from being used for employee testing, efficiency monitoring, or disciplinary observation in railroad and other industrial workplaces. Safety-sensitive jobs require concentration and trust, not constant aerial surveillance from management.

Using drones to monitor workers during testing or evaluations creates unnecessary pressure, distraction, and intimidation that can negatively impact both employee morale and operational safety. Workers should be judged on actual job performance and established procedures — not by being watched from the air like they are under suspicion.

If drones are used at all near railroad operations, their use should be limited strictly to legitimate operational safety needs, emergency response, or infrastructure inspection — not employee surveillance or testing.

I have been at CSX for more than 28 years and the flying of drones is a serious issue. The last thing a railroader needs, is one more thing to take his mind off of the job he is performing! There was a point where they(management) would let us know when and where they were flying. That doesn’t happen anymore. Who wants to hide behind or between equipment when having to take a bathroom break? The flying of drones is not for our safety either. They will watch you break a minor rule violation and keep watching, hoping you break a more serious one

The work we in railroad operations, switching, conducting, yard movements—is already one of the most hazardous professions in the country. Every second of attention matters. Introducing drone‑related monitoring or compliance requirements creates new distractions in an environment where distraction is deadly.
Drones should be left to professionals not railroad managers testing employees. They fly them over chemical plants which is not aloud but laws do not apply to railroad management.

I have 23 years on the railroad as a switch man and conductor.  I am very concerned about all the drone traffic I have seen in our rail yards.  I don’t believe it is safe. They fly low and have come close to our train many times.  They are a huge distraction. We are already required to perform the same amount of work with less people. We don’t need any more safty  distractions. The railroad is using the drones to watch us as well as inspect for rail cars and trailers. In my 23 years all the added electronics as well as drones have created distractions I would have never ever thought I would half to deal with. We move thousands of gallons of hazardous materials and yet the drones still fly over.  Please assist us and keep drones away from any railroad property. Make it a restricted air space please.

As a conductor of 13 years The unsafe actions of the railroads has to come to a stop at some point. With the distraction of these drones in the sky while we are moving 100s of tons of freight isn’t helping. They want us to keep a eye on the saftey of the yards, freight and other employees for terrorist activities while they give them all the tools to learn our yards, the way we operate, all the territory and even how to run a engine with the inward facing cameras. They preach it’s for our saftey that’s far from the truth it’s about helping them in court and studying us to cut jobs. They trust us to operate millions of dollars in equipment and freight let us do our jobs safely without continuing to add additional stress and worry to our lives. We just want to make it back to our families safely. Someone has to take a stand.

I think the drones are unsafe and distracting, it also allows the management to test you without having any contact with the employee, so there is no feedback on what we did wrong or right. A sometimes the angles of the video doesn't show the proper distance between equipment.

I have been a train conductor for 14 years.  Drones need to be banned over and near railroad property.  When I see a drone while working I wonder - is it operated by a railroad employee, a rail fan, or a terrorist scouting potential targets? Regardless of who is flying it, now I'm distracted and increasing the chances of making a mistake and hurting or killing myself or others.

15 year railroad employee, specifically in the yard for all of it. Railroads preach safety and to report suspicious activity all while using something to test us with that looks, sounds, feels suspicious. Would you allow a drones over refineries? Something like that would get reported instantly. Same should go with rail yards, when you cant tell the difference between friend or foe. Safety wise, ive tripped many times looking up because I would here it whizzing around. Railroads have taken what was supposed to help with bridge inspections to a fun toy that I can play with over rail yards.

Speaking from experience, having drones flying overhead can be very distracting with the noise or even just the sight of something flying overhead, and having a crewman's attention drawn away from what we are doing, where someone could accidentally stray in front of a moving freight car or be distracted while making a couple is asking for accidents and injuries to happen.

Drones are completely redundant and lazy. There are no uses they provided that the railroad doesn't already have.

I will start by saying I've have witnessed many different safety implications over my 21 years on the railroad. Many of which are met with initial resistance and after a "break-in" period become the norm and serve some sort of purpose. Drones however have never been welcomed by the men because they simply dont provide one once of safety for the men on the ground. They have only been used as a firing tool and a distraction to men working on the ground. Countless times I have sat on an engine and watched an employee staring up at the sky and looking for a small hidden object. Any distraction on the rail can be deadly so why are we continuously allowing these to be brought around the men on the ground?! Drones only contribute to the laziness of management not to the safety of men. They need to be stopped. Id be willing to bet since the implication of them they have not seen a decrease in human factor accidents but rather an increase brought on by one more unnecessary distraction on the rail. Please consider banning these overutilized firing tools from our place of work.
This is a huge distraction for workers in a serious industry where accidents are never little, or without injuries.

I have worked for Union Pacific Railroad for 15 years. Drone testing has become a more common practice by the managers in the field and also a higher up manager gets credited for a test if he observes it with him also. These test can be a distraction to the conductor in the field. A conductor should not encounter any type of distraction to while he is working since he is already working around an equipment that can cause at worst a death. These drones need to be illegal in any type of rail yard or main line so each and every employee that works for the rail road can go to their families safe

I worked for the Indiana Harbor Belt Railroad located in Hammond Indiana for 23 years. I was a qualified Trainman and worked within the entire district but spent most of my time in yard switching operations @ Michigan Ave. Yard located in East Chicago Indiana. Management used drones or Unmanned Aircraft for observations and rules compliance with no regard for the safety of the crews. This would take place above crews that were actively involved in switching operations, day or night and never mattered. I was literally switching and making a joint with a drone flying/buzzing not more than 20 feet above my head. I immediately stopped the movement and reported it as a safety hazard. This was never addressed and the IHB Police told us to watch out because there's more to come. Michigan Ave yard is located in close proximity to Gary International Airport located in Gary Indiana and I don't think they were adhering to the flight requirements around active airports.

This comment is in regards to Union Pacific Railroad's usage of drones to test TE&Y employees. When discussing with field managers on their compliance with FAA and FCC CFR's in relation to their use of drones they cannot provide me answers to some/all of the following questions. This makes me believe Union Pacific Management team are willfully breaking laws outlined by the CFRs. The only answers I get from them is that if Union Pacific tells them it's OK then they are OK. These field managers are not above the law and should therefore be personally fined for their willfully negligence and breaking of these CFRs. If they are truly Part 107 pilots then they are certified and trained on the CFRs they are violating. How will an additional CFR prevent their many violations dictated by Union Pacifics ongoing Drone Testing Policy? It probably won't change anything. Either way Union Pacific and it's management team should be investigated.

These questions are:

Are you a Part 107 pilot?
14 CFR § 107.12 Requirement for a remote pilot certificate with a small UAS rating.

Who is your visual observer?
14 CFR § 107.33 Visual observer

What is your drone registration number?
14 CFR § 107.13 Registration

Are you complying with Remote ID?
14 CFR § 89.105 Remote identification requirement
14 CFR § 89.110 Operation of standard remote identification unmanned aircraft.

Does your drone’s transmitter have an FCC Part 15 Certification? If
not, what is your HAM license FCC Registration Number (FRN)?
47 CFR Part 15 Radio Frequency Devices
47 CFR Part 97 Amatuer Radio Service

Did you perform a preflight inspection and checklist?
14 CFR § 107.15 Condition for safe operation.

Why was I not informed that I was participating in a drone operation?
14 CFR § 107.49 Preflight familiarization, inspection, and actions for aircraft operation.

Does your drone comply with Category 1, 2, 3, or 4 Operations? If not,
then you can’t fly that drone over me.
14 CFR § 107.39 Operation over human beings

What airspace were you flying in? If any airspace other than Class G,
did you get authority through LAANC?
14 CFR § 107.41 Operation in certain airspace
14 CFR § 107.43 Operation in the vicinity of airports.
14 CFR § 107.45 Operation in prohibited or restricted areas
14 CFR § 107.47 Flight restrictions in the proximity of certain areas designated by notice
to airmen.

During night operations: Does the drone have an anti-collision light
visible for at least 3 statute miles?
14 CFR § 107.29 Operation at night.

Are you flying below 400 feet above the ground level ceiling? Are you
flying 500 feet below the clouds and 2000 feet horizontally from the
clouds?
14 § 107.51 Operating limitations for small unmanned aircraft.

Are you flying with a waiver?
14 CFR § 107.205 List of regulations subject to waiver.

As a switchman for nearly 10 years in the railyards, I can tell you the rails have enough dangers and hazards already without the distractions of drones flying around. When guys are worried about being spied on by a drone, and potentially fired from their career, there is a possibility of becoming distracted with even the possibility of a drone watching them. Normalizing drones flying over the thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals can't be allowed to happen, because we won't know when we are seeing an act of terrorism in progress vs. an MTO trying to conduct tests. These practices shouldn't be normalized around critical infrastructure. Not only for the safety of the rail workers, but for the community as a whole.

Hi, I have 35 years on Union Pacific as a trainman! The way they drones are super unsafe, as they get them real close to distract you, and we see them all the time. It’s very hard to focus on your job safely, when this is going on!

I have 21 years switching in rail yards. With the last year the drone surveillance has gotten out of hand. Just 2 weeks ago while walking on gravel, the director of safety and a road foreman flew a drone over head. I heard the buzzing and looked up and tripped almost falling on my face. After 10 minutes of them watching me, they drove up to tell me I did a good job. When I asked about almost tripping from The weird buzz above my head, I was told that I shouldn't have looked up....real safe. These drone have nothing g to do with safety, just surveillance and discipline warfare on us.

I’m a victim of what call retaliation because I reported an unsafe situation to my supervisor he in return had the drone pimp squad out the next day flying over me, not the primary situation, but me.now I go to my assignments each day in fear not fit for my duties because of the pressure this has put on me I’m all about safety but hiding in the bushes and now in the air where does it stop.ive been railroading for 23 years and  never had an accident and never left from home with the intention of doing wrong. They have made the surroundings more unsafe and in predictable.all the things a conductor has to keep up with and now watching over me head in some very dangerous situations and industrial plants.we are needing this brought to everyone’s attention before there is a fatal incident

Hi my name is Tyler mayhan been a conductor for almost 5 years now and drone operations is exceptionally dangerous in yards considering your already under enough scrutiny and enough pressure while in the yard. There is 0 reason why drones are needed if any one of you have been to a yard you would see they are already filled with cameras.

Drones are a big distraction. One you are always feeling scrutinized like someone is watching. This does affect some people's ptsd. Also if you see it during a shove it takes your mind off of what you should be focused on. Drones also can fail and crash. If it was to hit someone from 400 to 500 feet in a free fall, it could and would cause a life changing event.

I am a train crew employee with 2 yrs of experience.
Active rail yards are already dangerous enough without drones creating distractions overhead.
Frontline railroad workers are the true experts on rail safety.
Drones used for surveillance and operational testing create unnecessary hazards and security concerns.
Railroad workers deserve to be consulted before these practices are normalized in critical infrastructure environments.
Normalizing drones flying over the thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals can't be allowed to happen, because we won't know when we are seeing an act of terrorism in progress vs. an MTO trying to conduct O-Tests.
They are completely unnecessary. The railroads employ enough managers that they can get out into the field and do their observations in person.
